systems.name: ii-V-I Harmonic Cell and Substitution System
systems.summary: The central system of this book is the ii-V-I progression treated as an irreducible harmonic cell from which all jazz vocabulary radiates. Members are the three chord-quality roles (minor-seventh ii, dominant-seventh V, major-seventh I) plus their minor variants, and the cell is extended through tritone substitution (replacing V7 with a chord a tritone away, sharing shell tones), whole ii-V tritone pairs, and the altered-dominant spectrum. Movement between members is governed by guide-tone voice leading (each chord's 7th resolves to the next chord's 3rd), chromatic root motion produced by tritone substitution, and the natural-to-altered dominant shift device (Mixolydian to super Locrian immediately before resolution). Modification rules govern how any member chord is altered: the four tension tones (#5, b5, b9, #9) mark the boundary between natural and altered dominants; the 7sus4 equivalence (equals a minor seventh on the 5th) enables voicing substitution; and Jazz 101 family substitution permits exchanging any chord for another within the same family (major, minor, dominant). The pedagogical one-way gate — master chord tones before scales, master the cell before its substitutes — is embedded in the system's ordering.

members.name: ii — Minor Seventh
members.summary: The ii chord in a major key; Dorian scale; half-diminished (minor 7 flat-5) in minor contexts.

members.name: V — Natural Dominant Seventh
members.summary: Dominant seventh with no alterations; Mixolydian or Lydian dominant scale.

members.name: V — Altered Dominant Seventh
members.summary: Dominant seventh with one or more of #5, b5, b9, #9; super Locrian (melodic minor built a half step above root).

members.name: I — Major Seventh
members.summary: Tonic resolution chord; major scale or Lydian.

members.name: i — Minor Seventh (minor tonic)
members.summary: Minor tonic in minor ii-V-i; Dorian scale.

members.name: Tritone Substitute Dominant Seventh
members.summary: Dominant seventh rooted a tritone (three whole steps) from the original V7; shares 3rd and 7th with the original in inverted form.

members.name: V7sus4 — Suspended Dominant
members.summary: Dominant seventh with natural 4th replacing the 3rd; equivalent to a minor seventh built on the dominant's 5th; unresolved in jazz usage.

traversal_rules.name: 7th-to-3rd Guide-Tone Voice Leading
traversal_rules.summary: The 7th of each chord leads directly (or via passing tone) to the 3rd of the next chord across the ii-V-I motion.

references.quote_excerpt: tendency for the 7th of a chord to lead to the 3rd of the next one
fundamental move between Am7b5 and D7b9—the 7th of the first chord leading to the 3rd of the next

traversal_rules.name: Natural-to-Altered Dominant Shift (Mixolydian to Super Locrian)
traversal_rules.summary: A dominant seventh moves from natural (Mixolydian) to altered (super Locrian) immediately before V-I resolution to create urgency.

references.quote_excerpt: dominant seven chord to change from natural to altered before it resolves in a V-I direction

references.topic: Mixolydian to super Locrian scale switch
references.quote_excerpt: first played from a corresponding Mixolydian scale and then switched to the super Locrian

traversal_rules.name: Tritone Substitution — Chromatic Root Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Replacing the V7 with its tritone substitute converts circle-of-fifths root motion into chromatic (half-step) root motion to the I chord.

references.quote_excerpt: replacement of a chord with another one of the same kind rooted a tritone away
chromatic root movement to get where we're going

traversal_rules.name: ii-V-I Cell Replication via Tonicization
traversal_rules.summary: Any chord in a progression may act as a temporary I, generating a local ii-V-I cell that redirects scale and chord-tone choices to the tonicized key.

references.quote_excerpt: ii-V-I in F within the tune

traversal_rules.name: Minor ii-V-i: Strong Preference for Altered Dominant
traversal_rules.summary: In minor ii-V-i contexts the V chord is strongly expected to be altered rather than natural.

references.quote_excerpt: for a minor ii-V-i, the tendency is strong for that V chord to be an altered dominant

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitute Shares Inverted Shell Voicing
modification_rules.summary: A dominant seventh and its tritone substitute share the same two-note shell (root + 3rd + 7th minus root), with 3rd and 7th swapped in register.

references.quote_excerpt: tritone substitutes of one another, and this is one place where they 'meet'
simple 7b5 chord is the place where a dominant chord and its tritone substitute meet

modification_rules.name: 7sus4 = Minor Seventh on the 5th
modification_rules.summary: The 7sus4 chord is equivalent to a minor seventh chord built on the dominant's 5th; allows Dm7 voicing and melodic ideas to substitute over G7sus4.

references.quote_excerpt: 7sus4 chord is very much like a minor seven chord built on its 5th
D minor seven ideas can typically fit hand-in-glove with G7sus4

modification_rules.name: Four Tension Tones Define Altered Dominant Boundary
modification_rules.summary: Alterations to a dominant seventh are bounded to exactly four tension tones (#5, b5, b9, #9); adding any of these converts natural to altered status.

references.quote_excerpt: four tension tones—b5, #5, b9, #9—and that's it

modification_rules.name: Jazz 101 — Same-Family Chord Substitution
modification_rules.summary: Any chord may be exchanged for another chord from the same family (major, minor, or dominant) to create embellishment without changing harmonic function.

references.quote_excerpt: exchange, replace, or substitute any chord with another chord from the same family
three types of chords, or three chord families: major, minor, and dominant

modification_rules.name: bVI Natural Dominant → V Altered Dominant in Minor Blues
modification_rules.summary: In minor blues, the bVI chord is a natural dominant and the V chord that follows is altered; the pair creates directed tension toward the minor tonic.

references.quote_excerpt: normal pattern of a natural dominant for the bVI chord leading to an altered dominant for the V

systems.name: Scale-over-Chord Selection and Reduction System
systems.summary: This system governs which scale a player deploys over any given chord type. Members are the principal scale/mode options arranged around the dominant seventh as the central battleground: Mixolydian (natural), Lydian dominant (raised 4th), super Locrian / altered (seventh mode of melodic minor), diminished (half-step/whole-step octatonic), bebop dominant, harmonic minor (over diminished-function chords), and pentatonic options. Traversal rules include the strategic reduction principle (group chords by key; apply one scale across a region, ignoring brief non-diatonic chords or emphasizing their chord tones), the three-fret equivalence rule for the diminished scale (one fingering covers four keys), and the pentatonic-within-diatonic revelation (four pentatonics hidden in a single major scale). Modification rules include the Lydian-dominant avoidance of the natural 4th, the super Locrian / Ab melodic minor enharmonic identity, and the explicit ceiling on scale-as-method thinking (scales are starting points that liberate initially but trap the player in boxes if mistaken for complete note-choice systems).

members.name: Dorian — Minor Seventh / ii Chord Scale
members.summary: Natural minor with raised 6th; used over ii min7 and minor tonic in minor ii-V-i.

members.name: Mixolydian — Natural Dominant Scale
members.summary: Major scale with flat 7th; default choice for natural dominant seventh.

members.name: Lydian Dominant — Unaltered Dominant with #11
members.summary: Mixolydian with raised 4th; avoids clash between natural 4th and chordal 3rd; accommodates #11.

members.name: Super Locrian / Altered Scale
members.summary: Seventh mode of melodic minor; captures all four tension tones; enharmonically identical to melodic minor built a half step above the altered dominant root.

members.name: Diminished Scale (Half-Step / Whole-Step)
members.summary: Octatonic scale with H-W alternation; covers four keys per fingering (three-fret equivalence).

members.name: Bebop Dominant Scale
members.summary: Mixolydian plus natural major 7th passing tone; eight-note scale favored by Charlie Parker.

members.name: Harmonic Minor
members.summary: Natural minor with raised 7th; applicable over diminished chords found within it.

members.name: Melodic Minor (Jazz)
members.summary: Major scale with flat 3rd; parent of super Locrian, Lydian dominant, and multiple chord types via harmonization.

members.name: Minor Pentatonic / Pentatonic Mixing
members.summary: Five-note subset; up to four pentatonic scales hidden within one major scale; overlapping positions usable simultaneously.

members.name: Whole Tone Scale
members.summary: Only two mutually exclusive scales cover the entire chromatic system; generates augmented and #5 dominant chord sounds.

members.name: Locrian — Half-Diminished / iim7b5 Scale
members.summary: Used over the half-diminished ii chord in minor ii-V-i contexts.

traversal_rules.name: Group Chords by Key — Single-Scale Region Reduction
traversal_rules.summary: Analyze a tune to group chords into key regions; apply one scale across the entire region rather than switching scale per chord.

references.topic: Grouping chords by key for soloing
references.quote_excerpt: we only need to apply one scale to groups of chords that are related to one key

traversal_rules.name: Non-Diatonic Chord Two-Part Rule
traversal_rules.summary: For briefly appearing non-diatonic chords, either ignore them and continue the key-region scale, or emphasize their chord tones as added color.

references.quote_excerpt: outside-the-key chords come and go quickly, you can choose to not pay attention to them

traversal_rules.name: Diminished Scale Three-Fret Equivalence
traversal_rules.summary: One diminished scale fingering pattern repeats every three frets and covers four keys simultaneously.

references.quote_excerpt: same exact patterns and fingerings occur three frets above and below

traversal_rules.name: Four Pentatonics Hidden Within One Major Scale
traversal_rules.summary: A single diatonic major scale contains three (or four, with #4/b5) minor pentatonic scales usable simultaneously over related chords.

references.quote_excerpt: C diatonic major scale contains three minor pentatonic scales within it

traversal_rules.name: Super Locrian / Ab Melodic Minor Enharmonic Identity
traversal_rules.summary: G super Locrian and Ab melodic minor contain the same notes; practitioners use 'melodic minor built a half step above the altered dominant root' as the practical mnemonic.

references.topic: Super Locrian formula and Ab melodic minor identity
references.quote_excerpt: Ab melodic minor scale contains the same notes as the G super Locrian scale

references.topic: Bb melodic minor common scalar ground
references.quote_excerpt: scalar common ground between these chords, as they are, is basically the Bb melodic minor scale

modification_rules.name: Lydian Dominant: Avoid Natural 4th Against Dominant 3rd
modification_rules.summary: Over natural dominant seventh chords, the natural 4th of Mixolydian risks clashing with the chordal 3rd; Lydian dominant (raised 4th) eliminates this clash and adds #11.

references.quote_excerpt: avoiding the natural 4th or chordal 11th, which could clash with the 3rd
avoids the potential clash of the natural 4th sustained against the chord

modification_rules.name: Scale-as-Method Ceiling: Scales Liberate Then Trap
modification_rules.summary: Scales are a starting point for note choice, not a complete method; treating any single scale as the total how-to for a chord creates a box of limitations.

engine_payload.ceiling_description: no single scale exhausts note choice over any chord or progression

references.topic: Scales as starting point, not ceiling
references.quote_excerpt: scales do not tell the whole story when it comes to improvising—not even about note choice

modification_rules.name: Bebop Dominant and Diminished Scales: Mastery + Restraint
modification_rules.summary: Both the bebop dominant and diminished scales must be mastered but resist overuse; they are high-demand specialty tools, not default choices.

references.quote_excerpt: Don't overuse this scale, but you should definitely master it
natural or major 7th as well as the b7th and was a favorite of Charlie Parker

modification_rules.name: Chord-Tone-Only Improvisation as Developmental Floor
modification_rules.summary: Chord-tone-only lines (arpeggios through the 9th) form the minimum required competence before scale-based improvisation; they are a stepping stone, not an end goal.

references.quote_excerpt: It is not an improvisational end-goal in itself
map out these chordal members on the fretboard and start to hear the chord changes

systems.name: Voicing Density and Comping Texture System
systems.summary: This system governs how chord voicings are constructed, selected, and texturally deployed in comping contexts. Members are voicing types ranging from full closed-position shapes through shell voicings (root + 3rd + 7th, then rootless two-string), quartal voicings, rootless extended voicings, and down to sparse guide-tone pairs or full silence (laying out). Traversal rules govern movement between voicing density levels: comping density is the primary dynamic lever, and the player moves from sparse guide tones upward through quartal and rootless to full textures based on musical context. Modification rules cover the dual root-string organizing principle (sixth-string or fifth-string root for minimal position shifts), the formula-based chord-family modification system (raise/lower 5th or 9th to alter; change 3rd quality to switch major/minor), the chord-melody rules (melody always on top; melody within a held shape), and the Freddie Green straight-4 constraints (precise release timing, minimal voicing size, felt-more-than-heard volume). The system explicitly warns against treating the default chord shape as a patch for slash chord notation.

members.name: Full Closed Voicing
members.summary: Complete chord with root on sixth or fifth string; all chord tones present; basis for movable-shape transposition.

members.name: Shell Voicing — Rootless (3rd + 7th)
members.summary: Two-string voicing on middle strings containing only the 3rd and 7th; root omitted; designed for use alongside a bassist.

members.name: Quartal Voicing
members.summary: Stacked perfect 4ths, exploiting guitar's native string tuning; enables outside patterns and requires finger-rolling.

members.name: Rootless Extended Voicing
members.summary: Familiar chord shape with root on low string omitted; increases mobility; bass covers root in ensemble.

members.name: Sparse Guide-Tone Comping
members.summary: Only 3rds and 7ths voiced; minimal density; maximum space for the soloist.

members.name: Laying Out
members.summary: Complete silence from the comper; an intentional texture choice in musical conversation.

members.name: Straight-4 / Freddie Green Voicing
members.summary: Quarter-note pulse with small root-3rd-7th shapes, precise release timing, minimal voicing size, low dynamic placement.

traversal_rules.name: Dual Root-String Organizing Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Every chord is learned in two voicings — root on sixth string and root on fifth string — so the next chord in a progression is always nearby without large position shifts.

references.quote_excerpt: you can always have the next chord coming up in a progression close by

traversal_rules.name: Comping Density as Primary Dynamic Lever
traversal_rules.summary: Move through voicing density levels (sparse guide tones → pentatonic ideas → full voicings → laying out) as the primary means of shaping intensity through a tune.

references.quote_excerpt: vary the density, and with it the intensity, of our comping to fit different musical situations
if we want to allow the texture to be on the thin side, we could go as far as to lay out entirely

traversal_rules.name: Movable Shape Transposition Across 12 Keys
traversal_rules.summary: Any non-open-string voicing is moved fret by fret to transpose to other keys, exactly as barre chords are shifted.

references.quote_excerpt: all chord shapes are movable when you're not using open-string voicings

modification_rules.name: Formula-Based Chord Modification Within and Across Families
modification_rules.summary: Starting from a dominant seventh formula (1-3-5-b7), raise or lower the 5th or 9th to alter; flatten the 3rd to shift to minor family; raise the 7th to shift to major family.

references.quote_excerpt: If we study the A7 chord and its formula, we can change it and create other chords
raise or lower the 5th or the 9th note by a half step

modification_rules.name: Shell Voicing: Root Omission for Compactness
modification_rules.summary: Full chord voicings are reduced to 3rd + 7th by removing the root, producing compact two-string middle-string shapes suitable for use over a walking bass.

references.quote_excerpt: leaving off the root, to boot ... very compact, easy-to-play, and flexible little two-string voicings

modification_rules.name: Chord Melody: Melody Always on Top
modification_rules.summary: In chord-melody playing the melody note must always be the highest-pitched note in the voicing.

references.topic: Chord melody rule: melody on top
references.quote_excerpt: always have the melody of the tune or song on top of the chords as the highest sounding pitch

modification_rules.name: Straight-4 (Freddie Green) Voicing Density Constraints
modification_rules.summary: Straight-4 comping requires minimal voicing size (root-3rd-7th or smaller), precise short release between quarter notes (not sustained, not chopped), and volume kept low ('felt more than heard').

references.quote_excerpt: moderate or minimal in size, and some small root-3rd-and-7th kind of chord shapes
'Felt more than heard' is the classic description of its role in the swing-era big band

modification_rules.name: Partial Voicings and Rootless Extended Chords
modification_rules.summary: Not all chord tones need be present; moving the root up a step generates ninth chords; rootless voicings are encouraged in ensemble contexts where the bass covers the root.

references.quote_excerpt: You don't always have to have the root or all the notes of the chord present
leaving them off is often a good way to vary the texture
